content_accuracy.notes: Content is largely accurate. Vector/matrix/tensor operations correctly explained. Complexity analysis correct. However: (1) Sparse matrix Quick Reference claims 'O(1) access avg' for map[int]map[int]float64 — Go map access is amortized O(1) best-case but can degrade; this is slightly misleading. (2) Tensor contraction complexity given as O(n^k) is vague. (3) Cross product listed as O(1) for 3D — correct but only for 3D vectors.

issues: Proper use of []float64 slices for vectors.
Correctly notes Go lacks operator overloading and recommends explicit functions.
Recommends gonum for production use — appropriate.
No language mixing detected.

missing_sections: Missing explicit 'Anti-patterns' section (required by AGENTS.md rule #8).
Decision Matrix uses English headers (acceptable, book is English), but format diverges slightly from prescribed template.
Section 29.4 (Matrix Computation Optimization) has its own mini-structure but lacks Definition header.

issues: Heavy use of <abbr> tags clutters raw Markdown and makes editing difficult.
Some verbose phrasing: 'blisteringly fast', 'brutally thrashes the CPU cache' is informal.
Quote attribution formatting is inconsistent — uses <strong><em> wrappers that may render oddly.
'See Also' links to Ch 34 (Polynomial and FFT) and Ch 39 (Bit Manipulation) — valid connections but somewhat tangential.

critical_findings: No severe algorithmic errors. Sparse map complexity claim is slightly optimistic but not critically wrong.
Cross product O(1) is correct only for specifically 3D vectors — should clarify.
recommendations: Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., using nil slices without checking len, encoding sparse matrices as dense).
Fix sparse map complexity claim in Quick Reference.
Clarify cross product is 3D-specific.
Reduce <abbr> verbosity for readability.
Consider adding a note about gonum/mat for production matrix code.

content_accuracy.notes: Strong coverage of Go concurrency. Parallel sum and map implementations are correct. Amdahl's Law correctly cited. False sharing explanation is accurate. Complexity analysis for parallel algorithms is sound. M:N scheduling model correctly referenced.

issues: Proper use of sync.WaitGroup, runtime.NumCPU(), channels.
Goroutine closures correctly capture loop variables via parameters — avoids common Go pitfall.
Correct hierarchy recommendation (channels > sync/atomic > sync.Mutex).
One minor: sync.Map entries in complexity table have '.' for lock/unlock times — unclear.

missing_sections: Missing explicit 'Anti-patterns' section.
Section 30.4 (Worker Pools) has Decision Matrix but some sub-sections skip it.
Quick Reference includes Context but it's not discussed in detail in the chapter body.

issues: Quote from Jeff Dean is thematically appropriate.
Good explanation of false sharing and cache coherence.
The hierarchical preference statement is somewhat opinionated but justified.
Some verbose phrasing ('massive amounts of RAM', 'harsh reality').
SIMD entry in complexity table ('Go asm') is impractical guidance for most readers.

critical_findings: No critical errors. The SIMD mention as 'Go asm' is more notable than useful but not wrong.
recommendations: Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., goroutine leaks, copying sync.Mutex, deadlocks).
Clarify or remove SIMD row from complexity table — not actionable for typical Go developers.
Add note about Go 1.22+ loop variable semantics making closure captures simpler.

content_accuracy.notes: SEVERAL CRITICAL ISSUES. (1) Section 31.5 'Password Hashing' code example uses SHA-256, which is NOT suitable for password hashing — this is dangerously misleading. (2) ECDSA verification at line 282: ecdsa.Verify(&priv.PublicKey, hash, nil, nil) passes nil for r and s parameters — this is broken code that would panic. Comment says 'placeholder' but should not ship. (3) Section 31.4 largely duplicates content from 31.3 (HMAC is fine, but ECDSA/Ed25519 are repeated). (4) The signature construction sig := append(r.Bytes(), s.Bytes()...) produces raw concatenation, not standard ASN.1 DER — warning mentions DER but code doesn't use it.

issues: Correct use of crypto/aes, crypto/cipher, crypto/sha256, crypto/hmac — good.
crypto/rand used correctly for key generation.
ecdsa.Verify call with nil params is broken — this is not idiomatic Go, it's incorrect code.
Password hashing section shows SHA-256 which is the wrong tool for password hashing — not idiomatic for security code.
Section 31.5 password code uses tabs inconsistently and sha256.Sum256 which returns [32]byte not []byte.

missing_sections: Missing explicit 'Anti-patterns' section.
Section 31.4 (Digital Signatures and HMAC) feels redundant — 31.3 already covers ECDSA signatures.
Section 31.5 (Password Hashing) lacks a proper Go example — the code shown is not password hashing.
Decision Matrix for password hashing is absent.

issues: CRITICAL: Password hashing code is wrong. The prose says 'use bcrypt/argon2' but the code shows SHA-256. Greatly inconsistent and dangerous.
CRITICAL: ECDSA verification is a placeholder with nil params — should have proper r,s extraction from signature.
Redundancy between 31.3 and 31.4 weakens the chapter.
Use Cases claims 'securing passwords via bcrypt' but the password section shows SHA-256 — contradictory.
Good coverage of timing attacks and constant-time comparison — these are valuable.

critical_findings: CRITICAL — Password Hashing (31.5): Code uses SHA-256 which is unsuitable for password hashing. Must use bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 from golang.org/x/crypto. As-is, this could mislead readers into insecure practices.
CRITICAL — ECDSA verification: ecdsa.Verify(pub, hash, nil, nil) will panic from nil pointer dereference. Must extract r,s from signature and pass them as *big.Int.
Redundancy: 31.3 and 31.4 overlap significantly on digital signatures.
The chapter title says 'Cryptographic Foundations Algorithms' but covers practical usage well — title is acceptable.
recommendations: IMMEDIATE: Fix section 31.5 code to use bcrypt or argon2 from golang.org/x/crypto.
IMMEDIATE: Fix ECDSA verification to properly parse r,s from the signature.
Merge sections 31.3 and 31.4 or clearly differentiate (31.3 = asymmetric encryption, 31.4 = HMAC only).
Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., using MD5/SHA-1, reusing nonces, comparing hashes with ==, using math/rand for crypto).
Consider adding a note about key management challenges (not covered at all).

Merkle tree odd-leaf duplication correctly implemented. PoW difficulty target and verification are accurate. Consensus table (PoW, PoS, PBFT, Raft) is correct and well-summarized.

content_accuracy.notes: BUG IN NON-NEGATIVITY CONSTRAINTS. The constraints {1, 0, 0} and {0, 1, 0} encode 'x <= 0' and 'y <= 0', which is the OPPOSITE of the intended non-negativity (x >= 0, y >= 0). The standard form requires -x <= 0 (i.e., {-1, 0, 0}) for x >= 0. Combined with the separate explicit check 'if x < 0 || y < 0 { continue }', the feasible region collapses to only (0, 0), yielding value 0 instead of the correct optimal (3, 1) with value 11. Knapsack DP is correctly implemented. Dual formulation is correct conceptually but the Go code is minimal (just prints). Background/philosophy of convex polytopes is accurate.

issues: Clean array-of-struct pattern for constraints ([3]float64).
Proper 2D slice allocation for DP table.
max helper function is correctly implemented.
No language mixing.
The solve2DLP function is well-structured for a 2D educational solver.

missing_sections: Missing explicit 'Anti-patterns' section.
Section 33.3 Duality code is a stub (just prints text) — lacks a real implementation.
Decision Matrix for 33.2 uses 'Use DP When' vs 'Use Branch & Bound When' — format differs from template.
Quick Reference includes Dual as 'Formulation' type — not useful as a quick reference entry.

issues: The non-negativity constraint encoding bug undermines the example — the code would give wrong results.
Background section on convex polytopes is one of the better conceptual explanations in Part VII.
Duality section is well-conceptualized but the Go code is trivial (just prints).
Warning about external solvers (GLPK, lp_solve) is good practical advice.
The Knapsack example is re-labeled as ILP — 0/1 knapsack is a form of ILP but this stretches the connection.

critical_findings: BUG: Non-negativity constraints {1, 0, 0} and {0, 1, 0} are wrong. x >= 0 should be encoded as -x <= 0, i.e., {-1, 0, 0}. Current encoding {1, 0, 0} means x <= 0. With the explicit x<0 filter, the feasible region becomes only the origin (0,0), giving value 0 instead of the correct optimal 11.
Chapter is called 'Linear Programming' but the main example is a 2D brute-force corner search, not Simplex. The title and content are somewhat mismatched.
recommendations: FIX the non-negativity constraints: change {1, 0, 0} to {-1, 0, 0} and {0, 1, 0} to {0, -1, 0}.
Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., ignoring infeasibility, unbounded problems, degeneracy/Bland's rule).
Consider renaming to 'Linear Programming Fundamentals' to set scope expectations.
For 33.3 Duality, either provide a real Go example or remove the code block and keep it conceptual.
Add note that Go lacks LP solvers in stdlib and reference gonum/optimize or external bindings.

content_accuracy.notes: Accurate coverage of polynomial operations. Horner's method correctly implemented O(n). Naive multiplication O(n^2) is correct. FFT correctly described as O(n log n) with Cooley-Tukey. Lagrange interpolation O(n^2) is correct. Non-power-of-2 padding note is accurate. The 'degree' measurement is sometimes confused with 'order' but usage is acceptable. Space complexity for naive multiply is O(n) — correct.

issues: Polynomial type defined as []float64 with method receivers — clean and idiomatic.
No language mixing.
Warning about lack of operator overloading is appropriate.
Good use of range-based loops.
Method receivers (Evaluate, Add) are well-designed.

recommendations: Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., not trimming leading zeros, using naive multiplication for large polynomials, forgetting inverse FFT scaling).

content_accuracy.notes: Exemplary. KMP, Boyer-Moore (bad character), Rabin-Karp, and Aho-Corasick are all correctly implemented. LPS array construction is correct. Rabin-Karp rolling hash formula is correct with primeRK = 16777619. Aho-Corasick failure link construction and search are accurate. Complexity analysis for all algorithms is correct. Boyer-Moore best-case O(n/m) is accurately noted. This is the most technically accurate chapter in Part VII.

issues: All implementations are idiomatic Go — clean, readable, properly structured.
Aho-Corasick uses [256]*ACNode array for children — appropriate for byte-level matching.
No language mixing whatsoever.
Edge case handling (empty pattern, m > n) is thorough.

critical_findings: No critical findings. The highest quality chapter in Part VII.

content_accuracy.notes: MISMATCH between description and implementation in 36.3. The section title and prose describe 'Metric TSP 2-approximation' using MST double-tree / Euler tour, but the actual code implements nearest neighbor heuristic. Nearest neighbor is NOT a 2-approximation — it has no constant approximation ratio and can be arbitrarily bad. This is a significant content error. Vertex cover 2-approximation is correctly implemented. Fractional knapsack is correctly optimal. Max-Cut randomized is correct (expected >= |E|/2). Set Cover approximation ratio H(n) ≈ ln n is correct.

issues: Clean Go code throughout.
Fractional knapsack uses float64 arithmetic correctly.
Graph type for vertex cover is well-structured.
Proper use of math.Inf and rand.
No language mixing.

issues: The TSP section is misleading — describes a 2-approximation but implements a non-approximation heuristic.

The 'Use Cases' section provides good real-world motivation.

critical_findings: CRITICAL — Section 36.3: Describes MST double-tree 2-approximation but implements nearest neighbor (which is not a constant-factor approximation). The code must either implement the actual 2-approximation or be re-labeled as a 'nearest neighbor heuristic'.
Knapsack 0/1 DP listed in Quick Reference as having ratio '1' — this is correct (DP is exact) but the Quick Reference header 'Approx Ratio' makes this confusing.
recommendations: Fix 36.3: Either implement the MST double-tree 2-approximation (build MST, double edges, Euler tour, shortcut) or rename the section to 'Nearest Neighbor Heuristic for TSP' and remove claims about 2-approximation.
Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., using greedy for 0/1 knapsack, applying approximation where exact solution exists, ignoring approximation ratio guarantees).
Add Decision Matrix for each subsections consistently.
Clarify Quick Reference header for exact vs approximate algorithms.

content_accuracy.notes: Excellent. Segment tree build/query/update all correctly implemented with standard recursive pattern. 4n array allocation is correct. Fenwick tree with 1-based indexing, i += i & -i for update, i -= i & -i for query — all correct. Space complexity (O(4n) and O(n)) is accurate. Decision matrix (segment tree for min/max, Fenwick for sum) is sound.

issues: Clean, idiomatic Go. No language mixing.

Fenwick tree uses 1-based indexing with n+1 allocation — correct and standard.
RangeQuery implemented as Query(r) - Query(l-1) — idiomatic.
Perfect use of Go slices.

content_accuracy.notes: All bit manipulation techniques are correct. Power-of-two check (n > 0 && (n&(n-1)) == 0) is correct. Brian Kernighan's bit counting is correct. Lowest set bit (n & -n) is correct. XOR swap is correct but outdated. Subset enumeration via bitmask is correct. Set/clear/toggle/isBitSet functions are correct. Edge cases (sign extension, overflow, precedence) are appropriately covered.

issues: Clean functions, good examples.
XOR swap shown but modern Go would use a, b = b, a — this should be noted as an anti-pattern or historical curiosity.
No mention of math/bits package (OnesCount, Reverse, etc.) — a significant omission for idiomatic Go.
No language mixing.
Uses int rather than uint for bit ops — the edge cases section warns about sign extension but using uint by default would be more idiomatic.

missing_sections: Missing explicit 'Anti-patterns' section.

Section numbering: 39.1 (Bitwise Operators), 39.2 (Common Bit Tricks), 39.3 (Bit Masking Applications), 39.4 (Decision Matrix), 39.5 (Quick Reference). Quick Reference is at 39.5 which is non-standard placement.
Some sub-sections lack independent Decision Matrices.

issues: Good balance of theory and practice.
The XOR swap is a classic trick but Go's tuple assignment makes it obsolete — should note this.
Missing math/bits reference is a significant oversight for Go developers.
Mechanical sympathy philosophy is well-articulated.
Edge cases (sign extension, overflow, precedence) are practical and important.

critical_findings: Missing math/bits package reference — Go 1.9+ provides built-in bit counting and manipulation functions that should be preferred.
No critical errors, but the omission of math/bits reduces the chapter's value for Go developers.
recommendations: Add 'Anti-patterns' section (e.g., XOR swap when tuple assignment works, using int when uint is safer, premature bit manipulation without profiling).
CRITICAL: Add section on math/bits package — OnesCount, LeadingZeros, TrailingZeros, RotateLeft, Reverse. These are the idiomatic Go way to do bit manipulation.
Recommend using uint for bitwise operations to avoid sign extension issues.
Note that XOR swap is outperformed by tuple assignment in modern Go compilers.
Consider adding a note about using bit arrays for space-efficient boolean sets.
